name: AI Agent Design Patterns
description: Core architectural patterns for building autonomous AI agents including ReAct, plan-and-execute, and reflexion
key_facts: ReAct agents interleave reasoning (thought) steps with action steps to accomplish tasks
Plan-and-execute agents first create a high-level plan then execute each step sequentially
Reflexion enables agents to learn from past mistakes by reflecting on failed attempts
Agent loops typically follow an observe-think-act cycle with optional memory storage
NVIDIA NIM Agent Blueprints provide reference architectures for building production AI agents
common_misconceptions: AI agents are the same as chatbots (agents take autonomous actions and use tools, while chatbots primarily generate conversational text)
More complex agent architectures always perform better (simpler patterns like ReAct often outperform complex ones for straightforward tasks)

name: Agent Memory Systems
description: Short-term and long-term memory architectures for AI agents
key_facts: Short-term memory stores the current conversation and recent actions within the context window
Long-term memory persists across sessions using vector databases or structured storage
Episodic memory records specific past experiences an agent can retrieve for similar situations
Semantic memory stores general knowledge and facts in an organized, retrievable format
Memory retrieval uses similarity search to find relevant past experiences for current decisions
common_misconceptions: LLM context windows serve as long-term memory (context is cleared between sessions; true long-term memory requires external storage)
Agents can remember everything from all interactions (memory must be explicitly managed with storage, retrieval, and pruning strategies)

name: Multi-Agent Coordination
description: Patterns for coordinating multiple AI agents including orchestration, choreography, and hierarchical control
key_facts: Orchestrator patterns use a central agent to delegate tasks to specialized worker agents
Peer-to-peer patterns allow agents to communicate directly without a central coordinator
Hierarchical multi-agent systems organize agents in manager-worker trees for complex workflows
Message passing protocols define how agents exchange information and coordinate actions
Shared blackboard architectures let multiple agents read from and write to a common knowledge store
common_misconceptions: More agents always solve problems faster (coordination overhead can make multi-agent systems slower than a single capable agent)
All agents in a multi-agent system need the same LLM backend (different agents can use different models optimized for their specific tasks)

name: Agent Communication Protocols
description: Methods and protocols for inter-agent communication in multi-agent systems
key_facts: Structured message formats ensure agents can parse and understand communications from other agents
Asynchronous messaging enables agents to work independently and process messages when ready
Event-driven architectures trigger agent actions based on events from other agents or external systems
Agent registries track available agents and their capabilities for dynamic task routing
Conversation threading maintains context across multi-turn interactions between agents
common_misconceptions: Natural language is always the best format for agent-to-agent communication (structured formats like JSON are more reliable and parseable)
Synchronous communication is required for multi-agent coordination (asynchronous patterns often provide better scalability)

name: Tool Calling and Function Integration
description: Integrating external tools, APIs, and functions into AI agent workflows
key_facts: Function calling allows LLMs to generate structured arguments for predefined tool functions
Tool descriptions in system prompts inform the agent about available tools and their parameters
JSON schema definitions specify the expected input format for each tool function
Tool results are fed back into the agent's context for further reasoning and action
NVIDIA NIM supports function calling APIs compatible with OpenAI's function calling format
common_misconceptions: LLMs execute tool functions directly (the LLM generates the function call; the application runtime executes it)
Function calling guarantees correct tool usage (LLMs can still generate invalid arguments or choose the wrong tool)

name: API and External Service Integration
description: Connecting AI agents with external APIs, databases, and enterprise systems
key_facts: API wrappers convert external service APIs into tool descriptions the agent can understand and use
Authentication and rate limiting must be handled by the tool execution layer, not the LLM
Database tools enable agents to query structured data using natural language converted to SQL
Error handling in tool integrations must gracefully communicate failures back to the agent for replanning
Sandboxed code execution environments allow agents to write and run code safely
common_misconceptions: Agents should have unrestricted API access (principle of least privilege should limit what APIs and operations agents can access)
Tool integration only requires an API key (proper error handling, retry logic, and response parsing are essential)

name: Chain-of-Thought and Structured Reasoning
description: Reasoning techniques that enable AI agents to solve complex multi-step problems
key_facts: Chain-of-thought prompting improves agent reasoning by generating intermediate steps before the final answer
Tree-of-thought extends CoT by exploring multiple reasoning paths and selecting the best one
Self-consistency samples multiple reasoning chains and takes the majority vote as the final answer
Decomposition breaks complex problems into simpler sub-problems that agents can solve independently
Reasoning traces provide interpretability by showing how an agent arrived at its decisions
common_misconceptions: Chain-of-thought guarantees correct reasoning (the model can still make logical errors within its reasoning chain)
More reasoning steps always lead to better answers (excessive reasoning can introduce compounding errors)

name: Agent Planning and Search
description: Planning algorithms and search strategies for AI agents to achieve goals
key_facts: Task decomposition planning breaks high-level goals into ordered sub-tasks with dependencies
Replanning dynamically adjusts the plan when initial steps fail or new information is discovered
Heuristic search guides agents toward promising actions based on estimated value or success probability
Backtracking allows agents to undo actions and try alternative approaches when stuck
World models help agents simulate the outcomes of potential actions before executing them
common_misconceptions: Static plans are sufficient for complex tasks (real-world tasks require dynamic replanning based on execution results)
LLMs can always generate optimal plans (LLMs may produce plausible but suboptimal or infeasible plans)

name: AI Agent Guardrails and Safety
description: Safety mechanisms and guardrails for controlling AI agent behavior
key_facts: NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails provides a programmable framework for controlling LLM and agent outputs
Input guardrails filter and validate user requests before they reach the agent's reasoning engine
Output guardrails check agent responses for harmful content, hallucinations, and policy violations
Action guardrails restrict which tools and operations an agent is allowed to execute
Topical guardrails keep the agent focused on its intended domain and prevent off-topic responses
common_misconceptions: Guardrails completely eliminate all risks from AI agents (they reduce risks but cannot catch every possible failure mode)
Adding guardrails always degrades agent performance (well-designed guardrails improve reliability with minimal latency impact)

name: Agent Evaluation and Testing
description: Methods for evaluating AI agent performance, reliability, and safety
key_facts: Task completion rate measures how often the agent successfully achieves its assigned goals
Trajectory evaluation assesses the quality and efficiency of the steps an agent takes
Red teaming involves adversarial testing to discover failure modes and safety vulnerabilities
Regression testing ensures that agent updates do not break previously working behaviors
Cost-per-task metrics track the computational and API costs of agent operations
common_misconceptions: Evaluating final output is sufficient (evaluating the agent's reasoning process and tool usage is equally important)
A single benchmark adequately measures agent quality (diverse real-world scenarios are needed to assess reliability)
